edge: Stochastic Gradient Descent is a key component in deep learning for enhancing performance and stability of artificial neural network training.
Deep learning involves optimization techniques such as Stochastic Gradient Descent to improve training dynamics.
Stochastic Gradient Descent utilizes information from the loss landscape to improve network training.
The paper explores the relationship between the curvature of the loss landscape and chaotic dynamics in neural network training.
The negative eigenspectrum of the loss landscape affects SGD performance and its removal can impact training dynamics.
Theoretical connection between chaotic dynamics and neural network training is explored to propose a modified SGD for non-chaotic training dynamics.
Empirical analysis challenges the conventional understanding of convergence in neural network training dynamics.
Chaotic network behavior in neural network training is studied, showing that it is mostly confined to the initial training phase.
Modeling chaotic network behavior involves a composition of a random walk and linear divergence.

The term 'chaotic' is used to describe the sensitivity to initial conditions in a system.
Lyapunov exponents are a tool to measure the evolution of displacement vectors over time.
Lyapunov exponents are used to characterize chaos in a dynamical system.
The Lyapunov exponents are calculated from the eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix.
The eigenvalues are defined as the Lyapunov spectrum of the matrix referred to as the Lyapunov matrix from now on.
Initial system perturbations evolve according to the Eigenvalue spectrum of the Lyapunov matrix.
A more rigorous definition of chaos requires the system's invariant set to be bounded.
The maximum eigenvalue of the Lyapunov matrix is also known as maximum Lyapunov Characteristic Exponent (mLCE).
The respective eigenvector of the Lyapunov matrix gives the direction of maximum expansion of an initial displacement as the dynamic system evolves.
The mLCE can be used to characterize three different types of system dynamics: chaotic, edge-chaotic, and bounded nearby trajectories.
A more fine-grained analysis can be obtained by considering the full eigenvalue spectrum of the Lyapunov matrix.
The biggest eigenpair of the Lyapunov matrix gives us the 'most chaotic' direction of the system evolution.

The system is assumed to either benefit or hinder the neural network training based on the presence of chaos. Chaos is considered detrimental or beneficial for training by manipulating the gradient updates through pruning the directions of maximum chaos.

The Lyapunov matrix is discussed in relation to chaos and its practicality in informing about the chaotic evolution of the system a posteriori due to considering chaos over the entire training up to the current time point.

A comparison is made between the Lyapunov matrix approach and the greedy approach to mitigate the problem of a posteriori chaos evaluation by considering a finite-time Lyapunov matrix after a single time step.
The eigenvalues of the finite-time Lyapunov matrix are known as Local Lyapunov Exponents (LLE).
The eigenspectrum of the Hessian is related to local chaotic dynamics, indicating locally chaotic training behavior in the direction of the eigenvectors of H.
The neural network with N parameters and its training behavior are discussed in relation to the Hessian and its eigenpairs, indicating locally chaotic training behavior in certain directions.
Positive eigenvalues imply locally chaotic training behaviour in SGD, while negative eigenvalues automatically lead to locally chaotic training behaviour.
The locally chaotic directions can be removed from the parameter updates in SGD by projecting the update vector onto non-locally-chaotic eigenvectors of the LLEs.
For a neural network with Hessian H(t) and eigenpairs, the gradient updates can have chaotic eigenpairs that can be removed through projection.
Training with SGD without momentum leads to updated training dynamics based on the update vector and eigenvectors, ensuring the system does not have exponentially diverging orbits.

A comparison is made between pruning the entire negative and positive eigenspectrum of the Hessian, showcasing that pruning the negative eigenspectrum has a far more detrimental effect on the training performance.
